regulation.scope: Any system that processes personal data of EU/EEA data subjects, regardless of where the processor is established.

rules.title: Principles relating to processing of personal data
rules.description: Personal data must be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently; collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes (purpose limitation); limited to what is necessary (data minimisation); kept accurate; stored no longer than necessary (storage limitation); and processed with appropriate security (integrity and confidentiality). The controller is accountable for demonstrating compliance.
check_hints: Personal data fields collected beyond the declared purpose of the feature
No data retention / TTL policy enforced in database models or cache layers
Absence of a data minimisation review: forms or APIs requesting more fields than needed
PII logged to application logs (names, emails, IPs, device IDs) without pseudonymisation
No accountability trail: missing audit log for create/read/update/delete on personal data
Data shared with third parties without a documented legal basis or processor agreement
non_compliant_patterns: SELECT * FROM users — selecting all columns including PII when only ID is needed
console.log(user) — logging full user objects containing PII
INSERT INTO analytics (user_email, ...) — storing PII in analytics tables without purpose check
No cron job or scheduled task to purge expired personal data records
rules.remediation_guidance: Implement a Data Processing Register (ROPA). Add column-level annotations for PII fields. Enforce retention policies via scheduled jobs. Replace full-object logging with structured logging that omits or pseudonymises PII fields.

rules.title: Lawfulness of processing — legal basis
rules.description: Processing of personal data is lawful only if at least one of six legal bases applies: (a) consent of the data subject; (b) performance of a contract; (c) compliance with a legal obligation; (d) protection of vital interests; (e) public task; or (f) legitimate interests of the controller, provided they are not overridden by the interests of the data subject.
legal_bases: Art.6(1)(a) — Consent (must be freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous)
Art.6(1)(f) — Legitimate interests (requires Legitimate Interests Assessment)
check_hints: Processing of personal data with no consent gate, contract linkage, or documented legal basis
Consent collected but not recorded with timestamp, version of privacy notice, and scope
Processing commences before consent is confirmed (race condition in async consent flows)
Consent bundled together with terms of service — not freely given or specific
Legitimate interest used as a catch-all without a documented LIA on file
Email marketing sent without explicit opt-in consent
Data subject's personal data processed after consent is withdrawn
non_compliant_patterns: sendMarketingEmail(user.email) called without checking user.marketing_consent === true
User profile created and data processed before consent confirmation callback resolves
Single checkbox: 'I agree to Terms of Service and Privacy Policy and marketing'
No consent record table or consent_log in the database schema
rules.remediation_guidance: Implement a ConsentService that gates all personal-data processing. Store consent records with data_subject_id, timestamp, privacy_notice_version, scope[], and ip_address. Handle consent withdrawal by immediately halting processing and propagating deletion/suppression signals.

rules.title: Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')
rules.description: Data subjects have the right to obtain erasure of their personal data without undue delay when: the data is no longer necessary for the purpose collected; consent is withdrawn and no other legal basis applies; the subject objects and there is no overriding legitimate interest; the data was unlawfully processed; or erasure is required by law. The controller must also inform any processors or third parties to whom data was disclosed.
check_hints: No erasure endpoint or handler (e.g. DELETE /users/{id} or AccountDeletionService)
Erasure handler deletes from primary table but leaves PII in: audit logs, analytics tables, search indexes, data warehouse, backups, CDN caches, or third-party integrations
Erasure is soft-delete only (deleted_at flag) with PII still readable in the database
No propagation to downstream processors — third-party services not notified on erasure
Erasure request queued but no SLA enforced (GDPR requires 'without undue delay', max 30 days)
Pseudonymisation used for erasure but a re-identification key is retained
No mechanism to handle erasure of data in event-sourced systems or immutable logs
non_compliant_patterns: UPDATE users SET deleted_at = NOW() WHERE id = ? — soft delete, PII still in DB
DELETE FROM users WHERE id = ? — primary table only, backup/analytics not cleaned
No call to stripe.customers.del(), sendgrid suppression list, or similar on account deletion
Erasure handler returns 200 OK without verifying all data stores were cleared
rules.remediation_guidance: Implement a choreography-based erasure saga that publishes a UserErasureRequested event consumed by every service storing personal data. Track erasure task completion per data store. Store only a pseudonymous tombstone record. Schedule verification that PII is absent from all downstream sinks within 30 days.

rules.title: Data protection by design and by default
rules.description: Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures both at the time of designing the processing system and at the time of processing itself. By default, only personal data necessary for each specific purpose must be processed — this applies to the amount collected, the extent of processing, the storage period, and accessibility.
check_hints: No pseudonymisation or encryption applied to personal data fields in database models
API responses returning full PII objects when only a subset is required by the consumer
Default settings expose personal data: public profile pages, opt-out rather than opt-in marketing
Personal data accessible to all internal roles without role-based access control (RBAC)
No field-level access control: all columns returned regardless of caller's permission scope
PII present in URL query parameters (logged by proxies, bookmarks, referrer headers)
No privacy impact assessment (DPIA) artefact linked to high-risk processing features
Third-party SDKs/analytics scripts loaded without data-minimisation configuration
non_compliant_patterns: GET /api/users returns { id, name, email, phone, address, dob } to all authenticated callers
router.get('/search?query=john.doe@example.com') — PII in URL
User object passed directly to analytics SDK: analytics.identify(user)
No @Encrypted or @Pseudonymised annotation on PII columns in ORM models
Role check absent: if (user.isAdmin) — missing for read paths on personal data
rules.remediation_guidance: Apply the principle of least privilege at both the API (response shaping) and database (column-level grants) layers. Use pseudonymisation for analytics. Annotate PII fields in data models and enforce encryption at the ORM layer. Make privacy-preserving options the default (opt-in, not opt-out).

rules.title: Security of processing
rules.description: Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including: pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of systems; ability to restore data after an incident; and regular testing and evaluation of security measures.
security_measures: Art.32(1)(b) — Ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience
Art.32(1)(c) — Ability to restore availability after an incident
Art.32(1)(d) — Regular testing, assessing, and evaluating effectiveness
check_hints: Personal data stored in plaintext in the database (no encryption at rest)
Sensitive data transmitted over HTTP (not HTTPS/TLS 1.2+)
Encryption using deprecated algorithms: MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, RC4, ECB mode
Hardcoded encryption keys or secrets in source code or config files
No key rotation mechanism for encryption keys protecting personal data
Missing TLS certificate validation (verify=False, InsecureSkipVerify=true)
Personal data in backups not encrypted with a separate key
No intrusion detection, anomaly alerting, or breach notification mechanism
Session tokens or JWTs containing PII without encryption (only signed, not encrypted)
No rate limiting or brute-force protection on authentication endpoints
non_compliant_patterns: password_hash = md5(password) — weak hashing algorithm
requests.get(url, verify=False) — TLS validation disabled
SECRET_KEY = 'hardcoded-secret-key-123' — hardcoded secret
AES cipher initialised in ECB mode — does not provide semantic security
jwt.decode(token, options={'verify_signature': False}) — signature not verified
db.connect('mysql://user:password@host/db') — credentials in connection string
No bcrypt/argon2/scrypt usage for password storage — plain SHA-256 used
rules.remediation_guidance: Use bcrypt, Argon2id, or scrypt for password hashing. Enforce TLS 1.2+ with valid certificates. Store secrets in a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Use AES-256-GCM for data at rest. Implement key rotation policies. Add anomaly detection alerts for unusual access patterns on personal data stores.
